items.title: Breaking Again
items.content_text:
**Sunday – 22:55**

Tonight, I broke again.
Collapsed.
Came apart.

And yet, I’m still alive.
My body remains, unchanged.
The world, with all its details, continues as it is, moving forward.
Time keeps passing.

I broke.
I screamed.

But even I couldn’t hear my own voice.
Let alone anyone else.

**May 18, 2026 – 23:05**
**Home, Groningen**
items.summary: Tonight, I broke again. Collapsed. Came apart.

items.title: The Inner Self
items.content_text:
**Thursday – 00:16**

For years, I felt the presence of another person living within me, someone who could have replaced everything I currently am.
Perhaps entirely different, worlds apart.

For years, I resisted his presence.
I fought him and prevented his emergence.

I was my own fiercest enemy.

All that time, I heard his voice,
his requests, his longings, his desires, even his protests.
But more often than not, I either ignored him or stood firmly against him.
Though at times, he would win.
I could even say that most of the time, he did.

Without realizing it, it seems I ultimately followed his path and pursued his goals, with one difference: everything was heavily suppressed, weakened, and expressed cautiously.

Now, lately, I have been deliberately trying to face this different inner side of myself.
I am trying intensely to make peace with him, to offer friendship, to show goodwill, and to make clear that there is now space for his presence and expression.

In truth, I am begging forgiveness from him, pleading for his return.

Please come back, and this time take full control.
I surrender, entirely obedient.

I regret all the harshness and constant obstacles I created for you.
I never fully trusted you, yet you were always right, or at least there was more joy and satisfaction in your desires and ideas.
More valuable experiences.
More enjoyment of life’s path.

I am remorseful, and now, in whatever way I can, I am trying to create space and regain the trust of this inner self, hoping I may be forgiven, hoping he may finally emerge with clarity and force.

I hope he does.

Never before have I been this eager to know and witness this inner self, and never before have I felt this ashamed and regretful before myself.

A sincere request for forgiveness from oneself.

I wonder whether others have felt something similar.

The path to reaching this point has been long, winding, and fascinating.
Had I consciously documented it, I might now have a thick and detailed book ready for publication.

It began with conflict against society, against the compulsory conditions of life, geography, politics, history, and everything beyond my control.

Gradually, it narrowed toward smaller territories: the city, the home, the family, the circle of friends.

The search for the root of inner pain, the guilty party, the cause of dissatisfaction, seemed to move from vast external scales toward narrower and narrower spaces, until now it has landed fully and decisively upon the self.

My constant struggle with society, country, circumstance, my enduring anger, and my belief that all dissatisfaction came from the tyranny of geography, eventually led to discovering familial faults, methods of upbringing, traumas, and unconscious mistakes from my mother and father that left deep inner wounds.

And finally, perhaps over the last year, I reached this point and understood:

All of that may be true.
But whatever exists, in essence and in the end, rises from within me.

I was no longer at war with external forces.
I no longer held indescribable rage toward society or geography.
I no longer carried inner bitterness toward my mother and her mistakes in raising me.

There was no one left to blame.

Not because those things were meaningless or without effect, no.
But because understanding their influence allowed me to see the course of life more realistically, to accept those forces, and to make peace with them within myself.

I still protest all that was unjust and wrong that happened to me and to us.
But I no longer seek battle.

For about a year now, that war transformed into an exhausting battle between myself and myself.

A struggle I fell into unwillingly, and the more I tried to control or understand it, the more unsuccessful I became.

Every complaint, every objection, every small or large dissatisfaction, I redirected immediately back toward myself and assumed myself to be the cause.

It is still somewhat like that.

Yet somehow, I do not know how or why,
it seems this battle may be ending.

At least I hope it is.

A battle I knew from the first day could have no victor and no loser, because both sides were me.

And now I see that all the ideas, all the works and projects I unconsciously created or imagined, were attempts to uncover this inner conflict, ways of becoming aware of what was happening inside.

Now the dots have connected.
A clearer image has formed.

Now it seems I must try to observe the image, to know it.

I was my own fiercest enemy.

An enemy not yet brought to its knees, yet one that has only now realized that the one it has been fighting all these years… was itself.

A painful and dramatic moment.

An image of the self standing before the self, both stunned, wondering why they treated each other this way for so many years.

**April 17, 2026 — 14:56 · Home, Groningen**
items.summary: For years, I felt the presence of another person living within me, someone who could have replaced everything I currently am. Perhaps entirely different, worlds apart.

items.title: Habit, Growth, or Forgetting?
items.content_text:
**Monday – 14:40**

I play my dedicated “lost-in-thought” playlist.
I try to slow down the passage of time.
To remain still behind my laptop.
To direct my main energy and focus toward my mind and thoughts.

To write.

I stare out the window of my room.
The weather is sunny, exactly to my taste.
I am immersed in the cave of my solitude, my safe room, longing and questioning why I didn’t take advantage of this good weather today, why I didn’t leave the house.
Yet at the same time, I cannot deny my need for solitude, silence, and giving space to my thoughts.

How painful it is that our desires and expectations, as humans, are never fully or sufficiently met.

In my mind, a highway of thoughts forms, moving at high speed.
My eyes see the world, but my mind sees things that have no physical or real presence.

I can remain immersed in this state for hours.
But at the same time, the expectation to extract something from these thoughts forces me to document this very state. To narrate it. To somehow use it.

I feel like a detective facing myself and my own life, constantly trying to solve mysteries, complexities, and find clues, yet in the end becoming more and more entangled.

I try to constantly place my entire life, past, present, and possible futures, in front of my eyes.

To observe.
From different angles.
Near and far.

This time, I am caught in understanding the meaning of “getting used to.”

A change of environment, distance, the absence of familiar and nostalgic people and places, does it slowly turn into forgetting and adapting to new conditions?
Or do all these dual emotions, attachments, and connections remain like a thread tied to me forever, and I am simply trying not to look back?

It feels as if we must accept that absence from a certain space or condition naturally creates emotional distance.
But at the same time, I don’t want that.

As someone who has deliberately spent much of life stepping out of my comfort zone, and each time I felt safety and ease, chose again to disrupt it and seek a new challenge, I must say:
this process is infinitely heavy and difficult.

It does not matter how many times, how many years, or how much you have experienced it.
Leaving the comfort zone will always remain endlessly challenging.

Now I am in a conflict, trying to hold onto the threads of the past, dragging them with me, while at the same time seeing this effort as irrational and futile, repeatedly reminding myself:
you have to let go,
no matter how hard or bitter,
you have to let go and accept that you consciously chose this position, and nothing is as it was before. You cannot carry everything with you.

Is the result of time passing simply getting used to things, or becoming more mature and stronger?
Does getting used to mean becoming colder, more numb, forgetting?

Is
forgetting,
release,
the fading of emotions, attachments, and feelings, and rebuilding from zero,
a sign of growth?
Or is it
weakness, helplessness, resignation?

It feels as if one part of me decides, steps forward, moves ahead,
while another part questions, observes, analyzes, and critiques.

A constant tension,
which part is right in the end?
Where should I move forward, and where should I stand back and observe?

An endless struggle to see myself from multiple perspectives.

Here, I am more socially present than before.
I attend gatherings.
I have a more active and dynamic social circle.
I go out more.
I am invited more.

Here, I enjoy life more.
I am experiencing new and unique things.
All of this brings joy and nourishment to my mind and soul.

But thoughts and attachments from the past weigh on my shoulders at every moment.

Now that, despite all challenges, I have a happier and more functional life here,
I wish the few close and valuable people in my life could experience this alongside me.

That is why part of me constantly thinks about what I can do to bring them into these experiences,
a sense of responsibility that feels irrational.
And at the same time, I tell myself:
you have no obligation toward others,
you must focus on your own life and move forward.
This way, everyone will be happier.

Yet the feeling of guilt and selfishness does not leave me.

There was nothing I could have done, and there is nothing I can do.
And somehow, I find myself even undermining my current potential.

There is no expectation from my friends and family back home except success, happiness, and progress.

But I live each day with a sense of helplessness and guilt,
and unconsciously deny myself the right to live fully,
to experience life,
even to experience emotional relationships.

As someone cautious and detail-oriented in life and human relationships,
here I have become even more conservative and careful,
despite the fact that one of my reasons for migration was to confront exactly this pattern,
to become more bold and free in action.

It seems, as I had said before,
the reality and course of life are often very different from our expectations.

**April 6, 2026 – 16:21**
**Home, Groningen**
items.summary: I play my dedicated “lost-in-thought” playlist. I try to slow down the passage of time. To remain still behind my laptop. To direct my main energy and focus toward my mind and thoughts.

items.title: 13 Days Later
items.content_text:
**Thursday – 14:31**

Today is cloudy.
Yesterday was bright and sunny.

It has been 13 days since the last time I wrote.
Two weeks.
I did not notice the passage of time at all, and I cannot believe how quickly these 13 days went by.
Thirteen days into our new year, one whose arrival I did not even get to truly feel.

During this short period, once again, many things happened, events that I am still trying to process and understand, while new ones continue to unfold without giving me any pause.

My father’s birthday passed, as well as the birthday of another relative.
The birthdays of a few old friends.
A trip with the university.
And the migration of close friends who used to live here, near me, to another European country.
And once again, the experience of feeling even more alone.

The 13th day of the year, which has always been a meaningful, beautiful day in Iranian culture, is now passing in complete solitude, far from any celebration, far from friends and family, far from gatherings and nature.

Since my teenage years, I have had a particular sensitivity toward the number 13.
I have never found a clear reason or philosophy behind it, but the fact that my birth date, in the Persian calendar, aligns with the 13th day of the third month has always felt like an intriguing numerical coincidence.
It made me more attentive to this number,
more aware of it than usual in my everyday life.

Because of this, whenever and wherever I encounter the number 13, it brings me a brief moment of pause and reflection.

And now, today, on the 13th day of the new Iranian year,
once again I skipped my class.
Once again, I am submerged in thoughts and stress.
Once again, anxiety.
Once again, rumination.

An attempt to process and understand everything,
almost futile.

The volume of events and their weight feel beyond comprehension.
Concentration has become something almost unattainable these days.
No matter how much I try to give myself time, space, and patience, to see myself, to feel myself,
it is as if I not only fail, but become even more entangled, heavier, more complex.

Every direction I look reveals an endless sea of ambiguity, fear, and worry.
And I, on a floating piece of wood, do not know how long I can rely on it just to stay alive.

On one hand, I do everything that needs to be done, almost flawlessly, automatically.
On the other hand, I have almost lost my presence of mind, my ability to truly feel and grasp the moment.

I constantly suffer, yet I do not express it.
I do not even see much value in expressing it.

The challenges, worries, anxieties, and sadness are one thing,
but not knowing the solution, the path forward, the way to improve, is far worse.

I tell myself, ask for help, talk, express everything in your mind, no matter how dark or heavy.
But before I can act on it, I stop.

Why should others have to carry my burdens, my anxieties, my depression,
when in the end, they cannot really change anything,
and they themselves are already dealing with their own lives?
Would I not just be adding weight, maybe even guilt, to something they never asked for?

Nothing gets resolved.

The fear of an uncertain future still prevents me from enjoying the present,
this moment, this position, all the achievements and blessings I already have.

I am still full of life.
Full of desire to act.
Full of excitement to learn and experience.

But my mind, my fears, my thoughts, consume all my energy
and block productivity,
block focus.

Time moves forward.
Life flows on, mercilessly, waiting for no one.
It does not stop.

And whether I want it or not, I am condemned to continue.
I am not dissatisfied with continuing,
not at all.

But these tangled, disturbing thoughts make everything infinitely harder.

Despite all these personal and external struggles, I continue.
I still try to laugh, to joke, to enjoy moments, to be helpful in society and among friends.
I still try to be useful, reliable, a source of warmth.
Cheerful, light, energetic.

Even though inside, I carry a storm.
Even though from the outside, I receive very little of that same energy back.

Still, I continue.

And at the same time, I try, deeply, to extract something from this path, from these struggles.
To say something. To create something.

Who knows where we will end up?
Who even knows where “the end” is?

No matter how much we plan or set goals,
in the end, it feels like we are all just moving forward to see what happens.

Thirteen days of the new Iranian year have passed,
and I still have not gotten used to naming this new year.

Many more sets of 13 days will pass,
and I will still be in the process of trying to understand myself,
life,
and how it all works.

**2 April 2026 – 15:15**
**Home, Groningen**
items.summary: Today is cloudy. Yesterday was bright and sunny.

items.title: New Year, Old State
items.content_text:
**Friday – 11:26**

Moment by moment, we are getting closer to the Persian New Year.
Only a few hours remain.

For years, ever since I first imagined migrating, one of the most vivid situations I pictured myself in was this exact moment:
my first New Year away from home and family.

For years, I constructed different scenarios.
I saw myself in that moment, likely experiencing a strange blend of sadness, longing, and joy, sitting in front of my computer, neatly dressed, in a clean and carefully arranged room,
beside a Haft-Seen table I had set myself, connected to my family online.

Or perhaps, I had managed to travel back home for these days, to be there, physically present.

All those years, I imagined this moment in every possible way.
except this.

Alone.
Angry.
Sad.
Exhausted.

No Haft-Seen.
No sense of appearance or preparation.

And not even the certainty of being able to contact my family.

For the first time this year, I saw no Haft-Seen,
no Sabzeh, no Goldfish, no Hyacinths
and naturally, no New Year gifts.

Alone, in a safe, beautiful, and calm country, far from friends, family, and everything familiar.
In safety. In peace.

And yet, a thought keeps looping in my mind:

When will I next be able to return to my country?
And when I do, how much will still resemble what I left behind?

My favorite places.
Streets.
Cafés and restaurants I used to visit regularly.
People.

I find no answers.
And I hate not knowing. I hate uncertainty.

It feels as if my entire life is currently suspended in its most undefined, unstable state.

I have never felt this uncertain about anything.

There is almost nothing in my life right now that feels fixed or definite.
Everything I look at is ambiguous, unresolved.

Meanwhile, my country and my people are enduring some of the heaviest traumas and atrocities.
We had not yet processed the unprecedented repression and violence inflicted by the regime, when another war began.

Even describing the contradictory emotional states we are experiencing feels nearly impossible.

I have tried, many times, to separate events and analyze them, and each time, I arrive at helplessness.

I wonder:

How much can we endure?
Psychologically. Physically.

It feels as though life has turned into an experiment, to observe the limits of my own endurance.

The harsher things become,
the more I expect myself to continue
to survive creatively.

But I keep asking:

Until when?
To what extent does this elasticity hold?

What else can we experience and still continue?
What meanings can collapse in our minds, and still, we move forward, searching for new ones?

How many times will we ever experience this life, to be forced into enduring such weight?

Despite everything, each year, certain occasions, especially the New Year, have always been symbolic checkpoints for me:
a moment to reflect, to learn, to forget, and to begin again.

But this year, for the first time, that sense of “new beginning” is not automatic.
I am forcing it.

The things I feel resentment, anger, frustration, and helplessness toward
clearly outweigh the things that give me hope and motivation.

And for the first time, I am not afraid to say it.

I am not afraid to admit that the scale is heavier on darkness than on light.

I do not want to perform hopefulness just because today is supposed to be symbolic.
On the contrary.

I want to gift myself the courage to say, clearly and firmly, even to my small circle:
I am angry.
I am sad.
I am exhausted.

And I am not ashamed of it.

I am repulsed by slogans.
By symbolic phrases.
By hollow congratulations and ritualistic wishes.

Nothing is okay.

I feel trapped in a psychological battlefield between conflicting political, religious, and social ideologies and I am exhausted by all of them.

People who selectively choose which atrocities to acknowledge and which to ignore, based on their ideological preferences.

I am disgusted by all of it.

People who, because of their beliefs, become thirsty for the blood of those who disagree with them, who threaten, who justify, who ignore the wrongdoings of their own side while amplifying those of the other.

Death is death.
Crime is crime.

What difference does it make what justification stands behind it?

In every case, without hesitation,
any form of injustice is condemnable.

How can you not see this, when, in the end, it is always us, the people,
who pay the price?

Mentally.
Emotionally.
Financially.
With our lives.

I am tired of the repetitive cycle of
“may this year never return,”
and the illusion of hope projected onto a better year.

I am tired of the constant tension in my throat, sometimes spreading to my head, my entire body.
I am tired of suppressed anger.
I am tired of the overwhelming flood of news, propaganda, predictions, analyses, announcements.
I am tired of numbers, names, statistics.
I am tired of emotional instability.
I am tired of helplessness.
I am tired of constantly feeling like I should be doing something.
I am tired of being tired.

The Gregorian New Year came with grief and anger over state violence.
The Persian New Year arrives with grief, anger, and the anxiety of war.

And somehow, I came across this line of poetry:

“The delightful spring has arrived, yet the heart is not in place.”

**20 March 2026 – 12:45**
**Home, Groningen — the final hours of the Persian year**
items.summary: Moment by moment, we are getting closer to the Persian New Year. Only a few hours remain.

items.title: Entanglement of Contradictions
items.content_text:
**Wednesday – 12:58**

I am caught in a deeply strange contradiction.

On one hand, I profoundly need solitude.
From the bottom of my heart, I want to disappear from the stage of life.
To sink into silence and stillness, and not worry about any event, judgment, or thought.
To simply pause, and drift into reflection.
No one to know me.
No one to wait for me.
Nothing expected of me, no responsibility to carry.
Pure stillness and slowness.
Ample time to process events and my inner states.
Which, of course, is not possible.

On the other hand, the most toxic and harmful thing for me right now is solitude.

One day, I become intensely alone; the weight of the world’s sorrow takes over my entire being, and it leads nowhere—no resolution, no relief.
Even the knot in my throat refuses to break, so that at least some of the pressure could be released.

Thoughts, fears, anxiety, and grief consume me like a black hole, and no struggle or effort seems to offer a way out.

Another day, I become surrounded—filled with conversations and people.
Sometimes even intentionally, I say yes to every invitation and plan, just to spend more and more time with others, to avoid being alone, to keep my mind from being entirely under my own control.
In the end, late at night, against my own will, I return home alone, face my solitude, and with overwhelming exhaustion, think only of sleep and rest—so I can begin again tomorrow.

Crowdedness until the very last moments of the day.

Time passes more easily.
The mind feels lighter.
But less gets done.
Fewer things move forward.
There is less room left for personal work.

A strange contradiction.
A need and desire for absolute solitude, yet at the same time an awareness of its harm.

And still, I have not found my truly safe people, or a space where I can express myself fully, without fear or concern.
Perhaps I never will.
Perhaps I never should fully reveal my true self.

What would happen if others knew how much darkness, sadness, and depression pass through the depths of my thoughts and emotions?
Would they be afraid, withdraw, see me as weak—and leave me even more alone?

And yet, I cannot ignore or suppress my emotional and psychological needs.
So far, I have found no solution, no help.

The friends I have back in my own country, even through distance and digital connection, were to a large extent that safe space for me—easing loneliness and homesickness.
But for some time now, they have had no access to the internet, and I am completely cut off from them.

Tonight, without realizing it beforehand, it was Chaharshanbe Suri.
I am not particularly a person of rituals or special occasions, except for a few rare and meaningful ones.
But the fact that I had completely forgotten this day and its date was painful.
How far and how lost had I become, to forget such a day?
And how disordered and sorrowful everything must be, that this historic and memorable occasion did not even carry the feeling of its arrival.

Now, how difficult it is to explain this day—its meaning and emotional weight—to my new, non-Iranian friends, so that perhaps they might understand even a fraction of it.

How heavy and strange it is that we are in the final days of the year, and instead of joy, hope, ease, and a sense of renewal, we feel tired, sad, angry, hopeless, restless, and depressed.

Unlike previous years, this year I was not alone on Chaharshanbe Suri.
I spent time talking and being with many friends.
I was not caught up in work, projects, and deadlines like before—something I had always envied others for, while they celebrated and I stayed behind, working alone.

This year, I was not alone.
I was not at home working on a project.
And that, for me, was something deep and significant.
After years, on this night, I was outside, among friends—yet still unfortunate, still sad, tired, waiting, anxious.

How far can a human endure psychological and emotional pressure, and still continue?

Outwardly, I seem to function strongly, even powerfully, in this regard.

But sometimes I tell myself—I wish I didn’t.
I wish I wasn’t strong.
I wish I could break somewhere, let it all out.
I wish I wasn’t so skilled at hiding my inner state and emotions.
I wish I could collapse, cry, be held—
for a long time.

Without fear of judgment.
Without fear of appearing weak in the eyes of others.

I wish I were simply weak,
and didn’t have to carry this extra burden of holding myself together.

**18 March 2026 - 01:27**
**Home, Groningen**
items.summary: I am caught in a deeply strange contradiction.

items.title: Perspective Shift
items.content_text:
**Monday – 10:20**

Lately, I've been making a lot of gaffes.
I make mistakes.
Things slip out of my control.
They are forgotten, and in everyday terms, I mess up far too often.

Small, numerous, stupid gaffes and mistakes.
I-who always lived with full control over my life and myself, who tried to make everything move forward flawlessly and without error, without letting anything go wrong-now feel as if control has slipped out of my hands.

I forget.
I leave things behind.
I make mistakes.
I lose focus.

And these incidents still appear in my mind as a huge, unbelievable failure.
I blame myself harshly.
I tell myself: how is it possible that so many mistakes and gaffes come from me?
What has happened to me?

I don't accept it.
As usual, I am hard on myself and interrogate myself. I look for causes and reasons-what has led to this mess, this lack of control, and consequently these flaws?
This is unlike me!

Last night, a strange, stupid, and infuriating mistake came from me.
A direct hit from my own mental backgrounds and prejudgments.
Like a trick I played on myself.
A mental assumption I believed in and trusted, accepted without checking-until suddenly I realized it was wrong, how stupidly wrong I had been, how I hadn't checked the information carefully and had trusted my own presumption.

During last night's incident, with all my strength-alongside anxiety, anger, inner conflict, and irritation-I tried to laugh. I tried, and I forced myself, to see the matter through a humorous, memory-like lens.
I tried, instead of anger, swearing, and harsh self-confrontation, to laugh, and to imagine a situation where later I would tell this mistake to others with laughter.

Although the incident itself was infuriating and disappointing to me, this change of method and perspective-the attempt at a different angle compared to most of my past experiences-was very important and valuable.
Instead of continuing the anger and grappling with myself, I at least tried to laugh and to see the whole thing with a foolish gaze.
It was an important and valuable step forward.

**9 Feb 26
10:35
Upsilon – Home**
items.summary: Lately, I've been making a lot of gaffes. I make mistakes. Things slip out of my control. They are forgotten, and in everyday terms, I mess up far too often.

items.title: Wandering In Time
items.content_text:
**Wednesday – 13:33**

Last year, in these very moments and days, I could not form any clear image in my mind of where I would be at this exact time the following year.
I had stepped onto a path filled with excitement, motivation, hope, fear, ambiguity, and an uneven mixture of many other emotions.

I did not know what shape or course it would take.
Where I would be a year later.
In what coordinates, in what atmosphere.

Four months have now passed since my arrival in the Netherlands. The speed and the number of events have been far greater than I had imagined.

I am still in a state of shock and astonishment regarding my migration.
In this new environment, this new city, I am gradually settling in and dissolving into it, yet at the same time accepting and fully digesting the fact that I have now been living in a completely different geography for more than four months remains difficult.
Accepting and touching the present moment is often hard, and recalling and becoming aware of the path already taken is even harder.

On one side, there is a feeling of guilt and regret regarding some decisions, behaviors, choices, and longings;
on the other side, feelings of pride, joy, motivation, and hope.

On one side, regret for the many experiences I never had in the past;
on the other, sorrow for not being realistic and not appreciating the conditions, opportunities, and blessings of my own life.

It seems that in perceiving the realities of life in general, I suffer from a serious weakness and difficulty. I do not have a proper understanding of the course of my own life or the moment I am in, and I usually realize the quality and value of things only after a long time has passed-at which point anger and regret overwhelm me, wondering why I did not understand and absorb my position in that very moment.

With complete boldness, I thought only about leaving and escaping from the city, the country, and the people.
To cut everything off.
To sever it all and be free.
To escape.

Distance. Separation. Freedom.

I was completely certain and decisive that I would never feel homesick. I even mocked the very concept of homesickness.
I believed I had no attachment to any person, environment, or place, and that migration would be very easy for me-that I would never miss these people, these streets, these crowds, or their anxieties.

Much like what I did years ago when I migrated from Damavand to Tehran.
Years later I was filled with regret and sorrow and came to the realization that I had been wrong.

Moving away and migrating to Tehran, expanding relationships and progressing in life-that was not the mistake.
The mistake was the way I thought about it, the imagination and the intention behind it.

I lost certain qualities.
I weakened and destroyed various relationships.
A self-chosen distance.
And I regretted it.

Everything, in practice and on paper, went well.
Scientific progress, professional advancement, specialization, income-everything.

But long-standing friendships filled with memories and depth faded away and disappeared.
Better performance, greater achievements-yet often weaker spiritual and emotional quality.
Fewer attachments. Weaker ones.

The turning point came when one of my close friends died in 2020.
I had been distant from him for several years.
The regret for all the days and memories we could have created together during those years-but did not-collapsed upon me, and even now I have not fully digested it. The weight of it has never been lifted from my shoulders.

Now I think that once again I may have taken the same path-only on a far larger scale.

This time I fled from my own country and continent.
For progress, for growth, for expanding possibilities, experiences, and relationships-and for everything else.
Yet perhaps also out of deep exhaustion, emotional weariness, and disillusionment with the environment and the people around me.

What if this pattern repeats itself again?

What if in a few years I once again find myself wanting to escape the moment-tired of everything and everyone?

Now I see all the signs of homesickness within myself.
Unconsciously, at night I dream of relatives, friends, and classmates.
The same people and gatherings I once seemed to be counting the days to leave behind.

Those same streets and alleys-dirty and anxiety-filled.
That same numb feeling of driving behind the wheel in Tehran.

I wish I had valued those moments while I was in them.
I wish I did not make everything so difficult for myself in my mind and in my life.

I am constantly fleeing from routine, stability, and a defined course.
As soon as a pattern becomes fixed and repetitive, I do everything in my power to change it and disrupt it.

It is as if, unconsciously, despite all my fears and anxieties, I am always trying to push myself outside my safe zone in any possible way.

Apparently this is an important, valuable, even beneficial trait.
And in truth, it has been.

If I had not possessed the courage-despite all my inner fears and anxieties-to step outside my safe zone and walk into unknown paths, I would not be here now. I would not have done these things. I would not have lived through my particular past experiences.

But I still question it.

Perhaps sometimes a safe zone is necessary.
Perhaps stability also has its value.
Perhaps it is good for a person to pause for a moment and rest within a healthy and secure framework, and enjoy it-rather than destroying it in hope of discovering greater joy and excitement in the next stage.

Whatever the case, I am homesick.

Filled with regret that before leaving my country and my city, instead of rushing toward departure and thinking only about getting out, I had paused for a while and enjoyed my final moments more-being beside my family, my friends, my city, and my country.

Not that I necessarily would have done anything special.
Simply that mentally and emotionally I would have been more aware, and allowed the moments to pass more slowly within my mind-processing and absorbing them.

I would have felt my family, my friends, and my beloved more deeply.
I would have sat beside them with greater presence of mind and spirit.

I regret nothing.

Everything I have done, I have done willingly.

What disappoints me is only my lack of proper mastery over my emotions, my inner state, and my perspective toward life.

Where will this path lead?
What will happen?

I do not know.
And I cannot imagine it.

It feels as if I have already reached and done whatever needed to be done, and now-rather than looking toward the future and its next stages-I find myself looking back at the past.

Perhaps it is a characteristic of this environment: concerns about the future are weaker here than they were in my own country, and now I have unintentionally found space to look more deeply into the past.

I am homesick and alone.
A deep, inner loneliness.
Homesick for myself, and for the moments that passed-spent anxiously rushing toward the future without recognizing their true quality.

**31 December 2025 – 14:18**
**McDonald’s, Atoomweg, Groningen – the last day of 2025**
items.summary: Last year, in these very moments and days, I could not form any clear image in my mind of where I would be at this exact time the following year. I had stepped onto a path filled with excitement, motivation, hope, fear, ambiguity, and an uneven mixture of many other emotions.

items.title: Imagination, Illusion, Reality
items.content_text:
I realized that the reality of life is very different from the images and assumptions I had in my mind.
As time passes, I understand more and more that many of the ideas I had about my future, my life, and the events that might happen were far from realistic.

Life unfolds in a way that constantly shows me how unrealistic and somewhat fantastical my thoughts about the future were.
I used to imagine that after a certain period of time, certain things would happen; that I would do certain things, behave in certain ways. But no.

Once I actually find myself in the situations I had previously imagined, it becomes clear how mistaken those assumptions were, and how different the reality of my life and its trajectory is from what I had envisioned.
In truth, they were closer to illusions than to genuine expectations.

**October 22, 2025**
**20:17**
**Somewhere in the sky, between Sweden and the Netherlands**
items.summary: I realized that the reality of life is very different from the images and assumptions I had in my mind. As time passes, I understand more and more that many of the ideas I had about my future, my life, and the events that might happen were far from realistic.
